buyingChecks.title: Buying This CLC CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Injector seals replaced recently — check for no black death residue
* No cold-start rattling (timing chain tensioner health)
* Full service history with regular oil changes — the OM646 can reach 500,000 miles if properly maintained
* EGR valve and turbo actuator recently serviced
* No loss of power or warning lights
avoidIf: * Black carbon deposits around injectors — injector seal leaks ("black death") are developing
* Cold-start rattling and no timing chain history — budget for chain work
* No service history — the OM646 is sensitive to oil quality
* EGR warning light or loss of power — EGR or turbo issues likely
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, injector or sensor problems

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The CLC200 and CLC220 CDI are Mercedes-Benz's stylish compact coupés from the CL203 generation — powered by the 2.2-litre OM646 four-cylinder diesel. The CLC200 produces 122 bhp and 270 Nm, while the CLC220 produces 150 bhp and 340 Nm. The OM646 is one of Mercedes' most durable diesel engines — with proper maintenance, examples have reached close to 500,000 miles. However, it has well-documented vulnerabilities: injector seal leaks ("black death"), timing chain stretch, EGR valve clogging, and turbo actuator failure. With typical CLC values ranging from £3,000 to £8,000, an engine replacement is a significant investment — a well-maintained example with documented injector seal and timing chain history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare CLC CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz CLC200 / CLC220 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Injector Seals — Inspect for black carbon deposits around injectors ("black death" warning) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering for 2–5 seconds (timing chain tensioner warning)
* Service History — Including regular oil changes (critical for timing chain longevity)
* EGR System — Check for any loss of power or warning lights; EGR clogging is common
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any lag or hesitation may indicate actuator issues
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, injector, EGR or sensor problems
* Crankshaft Sensor — Check for intermittent starting issues; crankshaft position sensor failure is common
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz CLC200 / CLC220 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned CLC CDI engine typically costs £3,500–£5,500 supply only. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. A used OM646.963 engine costs on average £1,246 in good condition. A reconditioned engine fully installed typically costs around £1,800–£2,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your CLC-Class.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz CLC200 / CLC220 CDI engine reliable?
items.answer: Yes — the OM646 is considered one of the best Mercedes engines when properly maintained, with examples reaching close to 500,000 miles. However, it has well-documented vulnerabilities: injector seal leaks ("black death"), timing chain stretch, EGR valve clogging, and turbo actuator failure. With regular maintenance and proactive attention to common issues, the OM646 is a durable and reliable engine.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz CLC200 / CLC220 CDI?
items.answer: The most common issues are injector seal leaks ("black death" — soot and carbon buildup around injectors causing a chuffing noise), timing chain stretch or tensioner wear (rattling on cold start), EGR valve clogging (soot buildup), turbo actuator failure, and crankshaft position sensor failure (intermittent starting issues).

items.question: Is it worth replacing a CLC CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept CLC worth £4,000–£8,000, a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 50–100% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Against a tired example with significant body issues, the same recon may exceed our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained examples with documented injector seal and timing chain history command a premium.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz CLC200 / CLC220 CDI engine code?
items.answer: CLC200 CDI (2008–2011): OM646.962 or OM646.963. CLC220 CDI (2008–2011): OM646.963. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block. The CLC200 produces 122 bhp; the CLC220 produces 150 bhp.

items.question: Can I repair the OM646 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?
items.answer: The timing chain and tensioner must be replaced if worn. A preventative replacement costs £800–£2,000. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £3,500–£6,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.
